title: Registration Flow Security Quiz
description: Test your understanding of secure registration implementation.

options: Return different messages for 'email not found' vs 'wrong password'
Return the same generic message for both cases
Return HTTP 200 for both to avoid enumeration
Add a random delay before responding
questions.explanation: Returning different messages for email vs password errors allows attackers to enumerate valid emails. Always return a generic message like 'Invalid credentials' so attackers cannot determine which emails exist in your system.
questions.question: How should you handle login error messages to prevent email enumeration?

options: Storing them in SharedPreferences
Using flutter_secure_storage or Keychain/Keystore
Saving to a plain text file in app documents
Keeping them only in memory
questions.explanation: Authentication tokens should be stored securely using platform-specific secure storage (Keychain on iOS, Keystore on Android). flutter_secure_storage provides this. SharedPreferences and plain files are insecure.
questions.question: What is the secure way to store authentication tokens in Flutter?

options: Allow any redirect URL for flexibility
Whitelist specific redirect URLs and validate them
Use the referrer header to determine redirect
Don't use redirects, use popups only
questions.explanation: Open redirect vulnerabilities allow attackers to redirect users to malicious sites after authentication. Always whitelist and validate redirect URLs. Never trust user-supplied redirect parameters.
questions.question: How should you handle redirect URLs in OAuth flows?

options: Trust the client-side validation only
Always validate on the server, client-side is just UX
Skip server validation for better performance
Validate only on the first request
questions.explanation: Client-side validation can be bypassed by attackers. Always perform validation on the server - it's the only place you can trust. Client-side validation is just for better UX (immediate feedback).
questions.question: Where should input validation primarily happen?

options: Let attackers try unlimited times
Rate limit attempts per IP and per account
Block the IP after one failure
Require a CAPTCHA on every attempt
questions.explanation: Rate limiting prevents brute force attacks while not being too aggressive. Limit per IP (to prevent distributed attacks) and per account (to prevent targeted attacks). Progressive delays or CAPTCHA after several failures is also effective.
questions.question: How should you protect against brute force registration attempts?
